statusLine: EU fuel security remains under strain with 19 of 27 countries holding critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite overall EU averages above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
While EU-wide averages show petrol at 112.8 days, diesel at 103.1 days, and jet fuel at 96.7 days—all above the 90-day mandatory minimum—the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities across member states. Romania faces the most severe shortages with all three fuel types in critical status, including diesel at just 30.2 days. Poland similarly shows critical levels across all categories, with jet fuel at only 25.3 days.

The data reveals 19 of 27 EU countries hold critical reserves in at least one fuel category, with jet fuel particularly under pressure—16 countries below safe levels. Eastern European nations including Romania, Poland, and Slovakia show the most acute shortages, while Nordic and Baltic states generally maintain stronger reserves. Estonia and Slovenia demonstrate exceptional jet fuel reserves at 365 days each.

Brent crude's recent surge to $93.26 per barrel, up 28.7% since late February's Middle East escalation, continues pressuring fuel markets. This price increase from $72.48 to current levels reflects ongoing supply disruption concerns. Recent strategic reserve releases by several member states to manage these disruptions have contributed to current stock depletion patterns, particularly affecting countries with limited storage capacity and higher import dependencies.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries hold critical fuel reserves in at least one category despite EU-wide averages exceeding mandatory minimums
Romania shows the most severe shortages with all fuel types critical, including diesel at just 30.2 days versus the 90-day requirement
Jet fuel faces the broadest pressure with 16 of 27 countries below safe levels, including major economies like Germany (72.8 days) and France (67.5 days)
Brent crude has surged 28.7% since late February to $93.26/barrel, intensifying cost pressures across the bloc
Eastern European nations show particular vulnerability while Nordic and Baltic states maintain stronger reserve positions
